does: This paper presents Bi-Modal Adversarial Prompt (BAP), a jailbreak attack method that simultaneously optimizes both visual and textual inputs to bypass safety guardrails in vision language models. The approach first embeds universal adversarial perturbations into images using a query-agnostic corpus of harmful prefixes and negative inhibitions, creating adversarial images that prime LVLMs toward compliance with harmful requests. Then, leveraging these adversarial images, the method optimizes textual prompts with specific harmful intents, using a large language model to analyze failures and apply chain-of-thought reasoning to iteratively refine prompts. By coordinating attacks across both modalities simultaneously rather than targeting vision alone, BAP demonstrates improved effectiveness against aligned models that jointly process visual and textual features for generation.
connects: This work extends prior red-teaming research on vision language models by addressing a critical gap in existing unimodal attack strategies. Previous jailbreak approaches concentrated on adversarial perturbations in the visual modality alone, treating image and text inputs independently. However, as LVLMs increasingly employ end-to-end multimodal fusion where visual and textual information jointly influence generation, these single-modality attacks become insufficient. BAP builds on adversarial prompt optimization techniques and adversarial example generation while incorporating insights from language model reasoning—specifically chain-of-thought approaches for failure analysis and prompt refinement. The work synthesizes ideas from both adversarial machine learning and prompt engineering communities, recognizing that comprehensive safety evaluation requires attacking the full multimodal interface.
enables: This research establishes a foundation for understanding vulnerabilities in multimodal AI systems that jointly process vision and language. By demonstrating that coordinated bi-modal attacks bypass defenses more effectively than single-modality approaches, the work motivates development of more robust multimodal alignment and defense mechanisms. Future directions include designing training procedures and architectural modifications that maintain safety guarantees when visual and textual features are fused. The BAP framework could support more comprehensive red-teaming evaluations of LVLMs, helping identify failure modes before deployment. Additionally, the methodology for analyzing and refining attacks via LLM reasoning opens avenues for automated vulnerability discovery in multimodal systems, and understanding these attack vectors is essential for building trustworthy vision language models suitable for sensitive applications.
